<analysis>
Draft candidates:

1. Primitive Obsession: lines 20, 30-31. fields frequentCustomerDiscount (boolean), tempDiscountCode, tempOrderNote (strings) used as primitives.
2. Data Clumps: lines 23-27. fields firstName, lastName, address, phoneNumber, email appear together.
3. Temporary Field: lines 30-31. fields tempDiscountCode, tempOrderNote only set in constructor and not used elsewhere.
4. Shotgun Surgery: lines 58-86. multiple methods updateContactInfo, updateName, updateAddress, updatePhoneNumber, updateEmail all change the same group of fields.
5. Divergent Change: lines 88-101. methods notifyForPromotion, notifyForDiscount, notifyForNewArrivals all handle notifications but are separate.
6. Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies: lines 103-111. methods applyDiscount, applyLoyaltyPoints.
7. Switch Statements: lines 113-124. handleComplaint uses if-else chain on complaint string.
8. Speculative Generality: line 126-128. askForReceipt method appears unused (no calls in file).
9. Dead Code: lines 130-136. anotherUnusedMethod, yetAnotherUnusedMethod never called.
10. Long Method: lines 138-153. longMethod has 16 lines, many tasks.
11. Long Parameter List: lines 155-161. orderWithUnnecessaryDetails has 6 parameters.
12. Duplicate Code: lines 163-168. duplicateMethod calls bakePizza("Cheese") twice.
13. Message Chains: lines 170-174. chainOfMethods calls cleanKitchen (not a chain? maybe mislabeled).
14. Middle Man: lines 176-189. middlemanMethod delegates to middleMethod which delegates to realMethod.
15. Inappropriate Intimacy: lines 191-194. accessInternalDetails accesses this.pizza (maybe internal details of Pizza class? but just logs).
16. Refused Bequest: lines 196-198. refusedBequest method empty (but Chef is not a subclass? maybe intended as example).
17. Data Class: entire class has many fields and getters/setters? Actually no getters/setters, but many fields and methods.
18. Large Class: class has many fields (10+) and many methods (20+), >200 LOC? total lines 199, class body lines 12-199 ~187 lines.
19. Comments: lines with comments explaining smells (e.g., line 19, 22, 29 etc.) but those are instructional, not compensating unclear code.
20. Feature Envy: methods that use other class data? bakePizza uses Pizza constructor.
21. Control Coupling: not evident.
22. Lazy Class: class does many things, not lazy.
23. Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces: not present.

Pass B critique:

T1 Evidence:
1. Primitive Obsession: lines 20, 30-31 show boolean and string fields used for discount and temp data. Evidence: "frequentCustomerDiscount = false; tempDiscountCode = null; tempOrderNote = null".
2. Data Clumps: lines 23-27 show five fields declared together. Evidence: "firstName, lastName, address, phoneNumber, email fields declared together".
3. Temporary Field: lines 30-31 fields set to null in constructor, never used elsewhere. Evidence: "tempDiscountCode and tempOrderNote set to null in constructor and never used".
4. Shotgun Surgery: lines 58-86 multiple methods updating same fields. Evidence: "updateContactInfo, updateName, updateAddress, updatePhoneNumber, updateEmail all update overlapping contact fields".
5. Divergent Change: lines 88-101 three notification methods. Evidence: "notifyForPromotion, notifyForDiscount, notifyForNewArrivals are separate methods for similar notification logic".
6. Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies: lines 103-111 two methods applyDiscount and applyLoyaltyPoints. But need evidence of parallel hierarchies? Not present; these are just two methods. Drop.
7. Switch Statements: lines 113-124 handleComplaint with if-else chain. Evidence: "if-else chain on complaint string with four branches".
8. Speculative Generality: askForReceipt method lines 126-128 never called. Evidence: "askForReceipt method defined but never called in file".
9. Dead Code: anotherUnusedMethod and yetAnotherUnusedMethod lines 130-136 never called. Evidence: "anotherUnusedMethod and yetAnotherUnusedMethod defined but never called".
10. Long Method: longMethod lines 138-153 16 lines, calls many other methods. Evidence: "16-LOC method calling 12 other methods".
11. Long Parameter List: orderWithUnnecessaryDetails lines 155-161 has 6 parameters. Evidence: "method with 6 parameters: pizzaType, size, crustType, toppings, extraCheese, discountCode".
12. Duplicate Code: duplicateMethod lines 163-168 calls bakePizza("Cheese") twice. Evidence: "duplicate call to bakePizza('Cheese') at lines 166-167".
13. Message Chains: chainOfMethods lines 170-174 only calls cleanKitchen, not a chain. Drop.
14. Middle Man: middlemanMethod lines 176-189 delegates to middleMethod which delegates to realMethod. Evidence: "middlemanMethod calls middleMethod which calls realMethod, two-level delegation".
15. Inappropriate Intimacy: accessInternalDetails lines 191-194 accesses this.pizza (maybe internal of Pizza? but just logs object). Not clear intimacy. Drop.
16. Refused Bequest: refusedBequest method empty, but Chef is not a subclass. Drop.
17. Data Class: class has fields but also many methods with behavior, not a data class. Drop.
18. Large Class: class has 10 fields (count: pizza, busy, completedOrders, breaksTaken, kitchenClean, frequentCustomerDiscount, firstName, lastName, address, phoneNumber, email, tempDiscountCode, tempOrderNote = 13 fields) and many methods (count: constructor, bakePizza, cutPizzaAndPutInBox, deliverPizza, hurryUp, cleanKitchen, updateContactInfo, updateName, updateAddress, updatePhoneNumber, updateEmail, notifyForPromotion, notifyForDiscount, notifyForNewArrivals, applyDiscount, applyLoyaltyPoints, handleComplaint, askForReceipt, anotherUnusedMethod, yetAnotherUnusedMethod, longMethod, orderWithUnnecessaryDetails, duplicateMethod, chainOfMethods, middlemanMethod, middleMethod, realMethod, accessInternalDetails, refusedBequest = 29 methods). Lines 12-199 ~187 lines. Evidence: "class with 13 fields and 29 methods spanning 187 lines".
19. Comments: comments are instructional, not compensating unclear code. The code itself is clear. Drop.
20. Feature Envy: bakePizza uses Pizza constructor but also uses own fields. Not strong. Drop.

<analysis>
Draft candidates:

3. Temporary Field - tempDiscountCode and tempOrderNote (lines 24-25). Method: constructor, lines 24-25.
4. Lazy Class - Drink field initialized but not used? Actually used in orderDrink? Wait, field is this.Drink (line 28) but orderDrink uses this.drinkOrder (line 184). So this.Drink is unused. Method: constructor, line 28.
5. Feature Envy - orderPizza uses this.Shop.receiveOrder (line 33). Method: orderPizza, lines 31-34.
6. Inappropriate Intimacy - complain accesses this.Shop.casher.calmCustomerDown and this.Shop.casher.chef.cleanKitchen (lines 38-39). Method: complain, lines 36-40.

17. Divergent Change - notifyForPromotion, notifyForDiscount, notifyForNewArrivals are separate notification methods (lines 139-152). Multiple methods.
18. Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies - applyDiscount and applyLoyaltyPoints (lines 154-162). Two methods.

24. Data Class - class has many fields with getters/setters? No explicit getters/setters, but many fields and methods that just set fields (update methods). Could be Data Class? But has behavior. Probably not.
25. Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces - Not applicable.
26. Dead Code - drinkOrder field? Not defined, used in orderDrink (line 184). That might be a bug, but not a smell.

Critique:

T1 Evidence:

T2 Granularity:
- For class-level smells: Large Class should be method="Entire Class". Lazy Class? That's about Drink field, but Lazy Class is a class-level smell for the Drink class? Actually Lazy Class refers to a class that does little. Here Drink is a separate class, but we are analyzing Customer. The field this.Drink is an instance of Drink class, but the smell would be in Drink class, not Customer. So drop candidate 4.
- For others: method names are correct.

T3 Taxonomy:
- All smell_type names must be canonical. Check: Primitive Obsession, Data Clumps, Temporary Field, Feature Envy, Inappropriate Intimacy, Speculative Generality, Dead Code, Long Method, Long Parameter List, Duplicate Code, Message Chains, Middle Man, Shotgun Surgery, Divergent Change, Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies, Switch Statements, Refused Bequest, Comments, Large Class, Control Coupling.
- Categories must match: Primitive Obsession -> Bloaters, Data Clumps -> Bloaters, Temporary Field -> Object-Orientation Abusers, Feature Envy -> Couplers, Inappropriate Intimacy -> Couplers, Speculative Generality -> Dispensables, Dead Code -> Dispensables, Long Method -> Bloaters, Long Parameter List -> Bloaters, Duplicate Code -> Dispensables, Message Chains -> Couplers, Middle Man -> Couplers, Shotgun Surgery -> Change Preventers, Divergent Change -> Change Preventers, Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies -> Object-Orientation Abusers, Switch Statements -> Object-Orientation Abusers, Refused Bequest -> Object-Orientation Abusers, Comments -> Dispensables, Large Class -> Bloaters, Control Coupling -> Couplers.

T4 Non-redundancy:
- For each method, each smell_type only once. For constructor: multiple smells (Primitive Obsession, Data Clumps, Temporary Field, Control Coupling). All different smell_types, so keep all.
- For complain: Inappropriate Intimacy.
- For askForReceipt: Speculative Generality.
- For anotherUnusedMethod: Dead Code.
- For yetAnotherUnusedMethod: Dead Code.
- For longComplaintMethod: Long Method.
- For orderWithUnnecessaryDetails: Long Parameter List.

- For middleMethod: Middle Man.
- For accessInternalDetails: Inappropriate Intimacy.
- For updateContactInfo, updateName, updateAddress, updatePhoneNumber, updateEmail: all Shotgun Surgery? Actually Shotgun Surgery is about one change requiring edits across many classes. Here we have multiple methods updating contact info, but that's not exactly Shotgun Surgery. Shotgun Surgery: "one logical change requires edits across many classes/methods." The logical change of updating contact info requires changes to multiple methods in the same class? That might be Divergent Change? Wait, Divergent Change: "one class changes for many unrelated reasons." Here the class changes for contact info updates across multiple methods. That could be Divergent Change? Actually the comment says Shotgun Surgery. Let's examine: The methods updateContactInfo, updateName, updateAddress, updatePhoneNumber, updateEmail all update contact fields. If we need to change how contact info is stored (e.g., add validation), we must change multiple methods. That is Shotgun Surgery within the same class. So keep as Shotgun Surgery for each method? But Shotgun Surgery is a class-level smell, not per method. The definition: "one logical change requires edits across many classes/methods." The smell is that many methods are affected. So we should report Shotgun Surgery for the class? But the output schema expects a method field. For class-level smells we use "Entire Class". However, Shotgun Surgery is not a class-level smell in the taxonomy; it's a change preventer that can span multiple methods. The typical output is to assign it to the class (method="Entire Class") or to the method that is part of the surgery? The examples in dataset might assign to method where the change is required. I'll assign to each method that would need change? But that would be redundant. Better to assign to the class as a whole. Let's check the taxonomy: Shotgun Surgery is under Change Preventers. It's not listed under "Special markers" for Entire Class. However, in the dataset, Shotgun Surgery findings often have method as the method that is part of the surgery. I'll keep as separate findings for each method, but they have same smell_type and different methods, so allowed.
- For notifyForPromotion, notifyForDiscount, notifyForNewArrivals: Divergent Change. Each method is a separate reason for change? Actually Divergent Change: "one class changes for many unrelated reasons." Here the class changes for promotion, discount, new arrivals notifications. So each method represents a different reason? That's exactly Divergent Change. So keep each.
- For applyDiscount and applyLoyaltyPoints: Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies. Keep both.

- For entire class: Large Class.

<analysis>
Draft candidates:

1. **Data Class** - Entire class: Drink has fields (drinkType) and getters/setters (implicit via assignment) but no real behavior beyond logging. Lines 9-26.
2. **Comments** - Lines 1-6: Copyright/license comment that explains what the code is for, but the code itself is simple. However, this is a header comment, not explaining unclear code. Likely not a smell.
3. **Lazy Class** - Entire class: Drink has only three simple methods and one field, doing very little. Could potentially be inlined. Lines 9-26.
4. **Long Method** - Check each method: constructor (lines 10-12) is 3 LOC, createOrder (14-17) is 4 LOC, addToOrder (19-21) is 3 LOC, confirmOrder (23-25) is 3 LOC. All are short.
5. **Feature Envy** - No method uses another class's data more than its own.
6. **Message Chains** - No chains.
7. **Switch Statements** - None.
8. **Control Coupling** - None.
9. **Primitive Obsession** - drinkType is a string primitive, but used for drink type. Could be an enum. Lines 11,15,20,24.
10. **Duplicate Code** - The three methods all have similar console.log patterns. But they are not identical code blocks.
11. **Large Class** - Class is small: 4 methods, 1 field, ~17 LOC.
12. **Long Parameter List** - createOrder has 1 parameter.
13. **Data Clumps** - None.
14. **Temporary Field** - drinkType is set in createOrder and used later, not temporary.
15. **Refused Bequest** - No inheritance.
16. **Alternative Classes with Different Interfaces** - No other similar class.
17. **Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies** - No inheritance.
18. **Divergent Change** - Class changes only for drink-related reasons.
19. **Shotgun Surgery** - No.
20. **Dead Code** - All code used.
21. **Speculative Generality** - No abstractions.
22. **Inappropriate Intimacy** - No.
23. **Middle Man** - Methods do not delegate.
24. **Comments** (again) - The comment at lines 1-6 is a standard license header, not explaining unclear code. Likely not a smell.

Critique:

- Candidate 1 (Data Class): T1 Evidence: Class has field drinkType and methods that only set/get it and log. Lines 9-26. T2 Granularity: method="Entire Class", lines 9-26. T3 Taxonomy: smell_type="Data Class", category="Dispensables". T4 Non-redundancy: No other Data Class finding. Pass.
- Candidate 2 (Comments): T1 Evidence: Lines 1-6 are a copyright/license comment. It does not explain unclear code; it's a standard header. This does not fit the "Comments" smell (code so unclear that comments are needed). Drop.
- Candidate 3 (Lazy Class): T1 Evidence: Class has 4 methods, 1 field, total 17 LOC, minimal functionality. Lines 9-26. T2 Granularity: method="Entire Class", lines 9-26. T3 Taxonomy: smell_type="Lazy Class", category="Dispensables". T4 Non-redundancy: No other Lazy Class finding. Pass.
- Candidate 9 (Primitive Obsession): T1 Evidence: drinkType is a string primitive used to represent drink type (lines 11,15,20,24). Could be an enum. T2 Granularity: This is a class-level smell? Actually, it's about using primitives where a small object would be clearer. The field and its usage are across the class. Method could be "Entire Class" or each method? The smell is about the design choice. I'll set method="Entire Class", lines 9-26. T3 Taxonomy: smell_type="Primitive Obsession", category="Bloaters". T4 Non-redundancy: No other Primitive Obsession finding. Pass.

- constructor: no Long Method, Feature Envy, Message Chains, Switch Statements, Control Coupling.
- createOrder: no Long Method, Feature Envy, Message Chains, Switch Statements, Control Coupling.
- addToOrder: no Long Method, Feature Envy, Message Chains, Switch Statements, Control Coupling.
- confirmOrder: no Long Method, Feature Envy, Message Chains, Switch Statements, Control Coupling.

Final candidates after critique:
1. Data Class (Entire Class)
2. Lazy Class (Entire Class)
3. Primitive Obsession (Entire Class)

Check for duplicates: all have different smell_type, so okay.
